evidence: Completely off the cuff. Here's what I would be thinking about. I would be thinking about trying to capture every college graduate from today [...] as their career coach. Note taker, but like notebook, if you like.

The value they get in the first six months will be like... yeah. The value they'll have in six years time if they keep up with it will be wild. Those people won't just be users, they'll be like fans and advocates.
Academies have their local towns, regions, catchment areas locked the fuck down. And that is why you can reliably generate talent. If you aren't able to do that, you don't survive as an academy.

I've become so black pilled on the idea that we are so limited by the ATS and the realities and the practical limits of how we process talent at scale that it's hard to even conceive of a world where you could have actual insight into how someone thinks before talking to them.
Companies will never invest in true AI for resume scanning. Because why would you process a resource where the ratio is like 99 crap for 1% signal. And even that could be totally fake now.
We've got a new kind of like Head of Strategic Ops joining week after next. I'll get her to reach out and let me see who else I know at Handshake.

Honestly kind of my ideal scenario is just getting picked up by a company that has the wisdom to realize we will look like absolutely look like the good guys if we just give Careerspan for free to every college kid in the country.
Participants: Vrijen Attawar, Ivor Stratford (Morpheus Group)

source_display: The Quiet Part Problem as Market Opportunity
evidence: Employers have selection criteria they cannot legally state. The gap between stated job requirements and actual selection preferences is a structural feature of hiring markets, not a bug.
An Indian recruiter placing US engineers said: I have nothing to give these people besides the resume. All of these people look the same.

source_display: Response Rate as the Only Metric That Matters
evidence: If Careerspan's candidates respond at higher rates than other sources, that's sufficient proof of value. Everything else (matching quality, portal UX, candidate coaching) just ladders up to this single metric.

source_display: Talent Scanning as Core Value (Stack Distillation)
evidence: What we are is talent scanning, and that's literally the only thing that matters. That is the only problem we actually solve.

source_display: Pre-List as Trust Demonstration (Not Security Blanket)
evidence: I wouldn't think of it as a safety blanket. I would think of it as demonstrating our worth upfront... it gets these recruiters that have been burned by software and that are not super trusting of AI to see that it does, in fact, generate some names worth paying attention to.

source_display: Sora Video as Product Vision Artifact
evidence: Ilse's recollection of a generated Sora video—an auditorium full of candidates that empties to three people after clicking a Careerspan button—is a remarkably clean articulation of the value prop.
Superposition wouldn't bring users. They might not even bring significant revenue. But their logo creates a trust cascade. This is pure signaling economics—the brand IS the product.
V's counter to 'you only have 500 users' was essentially: 'Yes, but they're the right 500.' This inverts the network effects playbook.
Logan's biotech friend disrupted her industry by going directly to morgues instead of through middlemen. 'My middleman is charging me too much money' → go direct to source.
Handshake as the 'actual perfect acquirer' - existing product-market fit with career centers; Handshake has distribution to all colleges
Everything you just said about Superposition is also true about working with a recruiter specifically. Except we don't get to put Superposition on our website.
If I were to stack rank the moments of 'oh shit, this is really useful to me' from a recruiter perspective, it would be: I'm seeing people that would have taken me a while to find.
